name: Algarvio Portuguese
notes: Algarvio Portuguese, southernmost continental dialect (Faro, Portimão, Lagos, Sagres). Modelled features: (1) DEFINING Barlavento feature — fronting of the stressed labio-velar /u/ → [y] (tudo→[ˈtydu], lume→[ˈlymɨ], mudo→[ˈmydu]); encoded as the allophone rule ALG_U_FRONTING, stressed nucleus only (Cintra 1971; Brissos 2014; Segura da Cruz 1989). (2) /ej/ → [e] and /ow/ → [o] monophthongs (leite→[ˈletɨ], outro→[ˈotɾu]). (3) Southern sibilant voicing — word-final /s/ → [ʒ] (mas→[maʒ], muitos amigos→[ˈmũj̃tuʒ ɐˈmiɡuʒ]); encoded in positional_graphemes s/coda→[ʒ], word_final→[ʒ]. This is the STRONGEST realisation of the /ʒ/-for-/z/ external sandhi (word-final s before a vowel-initial word → [ʒ]; the 'Tajaver' pronunciation of estás a ver → [eˈʃtaʒ ˈɐ ˈvɛɾ]; Portuguese With Leo, 'The 8 accents', native-speaker source), shared more weakly by São Miguel (see pt-PT-x-acores, which applies it prevocalically only) and variable in Coimbra, while Lisbon and the North keep [z]. Because the Algarve realisation is categorical across all word-final positions, it is modelled by the positional word_final [ʒ] map (not a separate prevocalic sandhi rule) and therefore already surfaces [ʒ] before a following vowel. (4) Open stressed vowel qualities [ɛ, ɔ]. (5) Dark coda /l/→[ł] and coda /s,z/→[ʃ,ʒ] inherited from the pt-PT base allophone_rules. NOT MODELLED: the older/rural apico-alveolar sibilant [s̥] (articulatory, not phoneme-level simulable — Cintra 1971 flags this class of feature as non-modellable); the broader stressed [a]→[ɛ] / [ɔ]→[o̝] chain shift (context-conditioned; see ALG_U_FRONTING notes). No betacism: /v/ ≠ /b/. PROCLITIC GUARD: the common function words o/os/no/nos/do/dos/ao/aos/pelo/pelos/um/uns are given word_exceptions with their correct [u]-vowel forms (o→[u], no→[nu]); the word-level stress detector mis-marks these monosyllabic clitics as stressed, so without the override ALG_U_FRONTING would wrongly front the article/contraction vowel to [y]. Genuine stressed lexical /u/ still fronts (tudo→[ˈtydu], sul→[ˈsyɫ]). ACOUSTIC REFINEMENT (Brissos 2014b, 'A vogal u', AVOC): the Barlavento /u/-fronting is context-conditioned — stressed /u/ is NOT fronted after a bilabial onset (/p,b,m/), where it identifies with [u] (Praia da Salema: 25% of the F2 space after bilabial, 'muito mais perto de [u] do que de [y]'). Encoded as the guard rule ALG_U_NO_FRONT_AFTER_BILABIAL, ordered before ALG_U_FRONTING (so e.g. mudo -> [ˈmudu], not [ˈmydu]; genuine non-bilabial /u/ still fronts: tudo, sul). Brissos & Saramago (2014, pp.55-59, 65) corroborate the Barlavento-Algarvio (SW-zone) stressed /u/-fronting isogloss, aligning the perceptual-dialectology record with the acoustic data.

ancestors.notes: Standard European Portuguese; Algarvio diverges in sibilant voicing (s→ʒ coda/final), ei/ou monophthongs, and open vowel qualities

sources.author: Cintra, L. F. Lindley
sources.title: Nova proposta de classificação dos dialectos galego-portugueses
sources.publisher: Boletim de Filologia (Centro de Estudos Filológicos, Lisboa), 22: 81–116
sources.notes: Foundational classification of European Portuguese dialects. Delimits the Barlavento Algarvio by the isogloss of stressed-/u/ palatalisation (‘a palatalização, em maior ou menor grau, da vogal tónica u’) and lists final unstressed high-vowel deletion (‘a queda das vogais finais -u, -i (ou -e), outro dos fenómenos mais típicos desta região’) as a central-southern (Alto Alentejo / Beira Baixa) feature.

sources.author: Brissos, Fernando
sources.title: New insights into Portuguese central-southern dialects: understanding their present and past forms through acoustic data from stressed vowels
sources.notes: Acoustic study of central-southern EP vowel systems. Documents Algarvio stressed /u/ → [y] ([tˈyd] ‘tudo’, [lˈymɨ] ‘lume’) and final unstressed [u] deletion/reduction ([sˈœʃt] ‘cesto’); cites Lüdtke (1957) and Maia (1975–78) that the /u/ palatalisation is stressed-only.

sources.author: Segura da Cruz, Maria Luísa
sources.title: La palatalisation de [u] dans le «Barlavento Algarvio»
sources.publisher: Espaces romans (études de dialectologie et de géolinguistique offertes à Gaston Tuaillon), II: 434–456. Grenoble: Université Stendhal / ELLUG
sources.notes: Monograph devoted to the stressed-/u/ → [y] fronting isogloss of the western (Barlavento) Algarve.

sources.author: Portuguese With Leo
sources.title: The 8 Portuguese accents of Portugal
sources.notes: Native-speaker EP accent breakdown (NOT academic). Identifies the Algarve as having the strongest /ʒ/-for-/z/ external sandhi (word-final s + vowel-initial word → [ʒ]): 'Tajaver' (estás a ver), 'quijentrar', 'majaágua', 'muitojamigos', 'éjo número um'; shared (weaker) by São Miguel (Azores), variable in Coimbra, absent in Lisbon and the North (which keep [z]). Grounds the external-sandhi reading of the word-final [ʒ] this spec already models via its positional word_final map. A page-pinned academic source for the prevocalic-[ʒ] sandhi specifically was not located; the [ʒ]-for-final-/s/ voicing sits within the documented southern sibilant behaviour.

sources.author: Boléo, M.P.
sources.title: Estudos de linguística portuguesa e românica

sources.author: Brissos, Fernando
sources.title: A vogal u, os dialectos do Centro-Sul português e a dialectologia acústica
sources.publisher: Textos Selecionados, XXIX Encontro Nacional da Associação Portuguesa de Linguística, Porto: APL, pp. 85-102
sources.notes: Acoustic-atlas (AVOC) study of stressed /u/ across 13 Centro-Sul survey points. Refinement over Brissos (2014, JPL): the /u/-fronting is CONTEXT-CONDITIONED — after a bilabial onset (/p,b,m/) the vowel loses most of its advancement and sits near [u] (Praia da Salema / Barlavento: after bilabial u = 25% of the F2 acoustic space, 'muito mais perto de [u] do que de [y]', vs 64% after alveo-dental/palatal/velar, pp.87-88; Alpalhão / Centro-Interior: after bilabial u = 11%, 'identificando-se assim com [u]', p.89). Grounds the bilabial guard (stressed /u/ kept [u] after /p,b,m/). Also documents that the fronting spans [ʉ]~[y] (acoustically central, most often written [ʉ] for the Centro-Interior, [y]/threshold-of-[y] for the Barlavento, p.87) and that across the Sul /u/ is frequently NOT the most-backed vowel (7 of 13 points, pp.91-93) — kept to notes, not modelled.

sources.author: Brissos, Fernando & Saramago, João
sources.title: O problema da diversidade dialectal do Centro-Sul português: informação perceptiva versus informação acústica
sources.notes: Perceptual-vs-acoustic synthesis over Brissos (2014), mapping the Centro-Sul point by point. Corroborates the stressed /u/-palatalisation isogloss for the Beira-Baixa/Alto-Alentejo (CI) and Barlavento-Algarvio (SW) zones (§3, pp.55-59) and reports that across the Sul the stressed /u/ is realised fronted/central rather than as the most-backed vowel of the system (p.65); also records final unstressed <o> realised as a neutral schwa rather than [u] in parts of the Centro-Sul (p.57).

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam). DIPHTHONGS: declared so the bundled syllabifier counts nuclei correctly — without them every vowel run merges into one nucleus and co-e-lho is read as coe-lho, one syllable short, putting the stress and its vowel reduction on the wrong vowel. ⟨oe⟩ is hiatus, not a diphthong (Cunha & Cintra, §Ditongos).

allophone_rules.notes: Bilabial-context guard: the stressed labio-velar /u/ is NOT fronted after a bilabial onset (/p,b,m/). Brissos (2014b, 'A vogal u', AVOC acoustic atlas) shows the fronting is context-conditioned — after a bilabial the vowel loses most of its advancement and identifies with [u] (Praia da Salema/Barlavento: 25% of the F2 space after bilabial vs 64% elsewhere, pp.87-88; Alpalhão/Centro-Interior: 11%, 'identificando-se assim com [u]', p.89). Ordered BEFORE the fronting rule so it wins (first-matching rule fires): after /p,b,m/ the guard keeps [u]; in every other onset context the fronting rule downstream applies. Categorical simplification of a gradient effect (after a bilabial the vowel is 'much closer to [u]', not exactly [u]); the gradience is documented here, not encoded.

Barlavento Algarvio fronting of the stressed labio-velar /u/ to the front rounded [y] — the defining and most salient feature of the western Algarve. Cintra (1971) delimits the sub-dialect by exactly this isogloss (‘a palatalização, em maior ou menor grau, da vogal tónica u’); Brissos (2014) gives [tˈyd] ‘tudo’ and [lˈymɨ] ‘lume’; Segura da Cruz (1989) is a study dedicated to it. Restricted to the STRESSED nucleus (stress=‘stressed’): it does not apply to unstressed /u/ (Lüdtke 1957; Maia 1975–78, via Cintra 1971 and Brissos 2014), so the condition also blocks it from feeding on the inherited pt-PT unstressed /o/→[u] reduction. NOTES-ONLY (not modelled): the wider central-southern chain shift — stressed [a]→[ɛ] in high-vowel/palatal contexts and [ɔ]→[o̝] raising (Brissos 2014) — is context-conditioned and left to notes to avoid a cascade collapse of the vowel space; only the categorical /u/→[y] is realised here.

location.notes: the Algarve — Portugal's southernmost region (Faro, Portimão, Lagos). [Wikidata Q26831 P625]
